1	\chapter{Relative Picard sheaf --- \texttt{Scheme.Modules.tensorObj} substrate (A.1.c.SubT)}
2	\label{chap:Picard_TensorObjSubstrate}
3	
4	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
5	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
6	
7	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
8	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
9	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
10	\[
11	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
12	  \qquad
13	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
14	\]
15	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
16	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
17	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
18	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
19	
20	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
21	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
22	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
23	% groups)
24	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
25	% SOURCE QUOTE:
26	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
27	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
28	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
29	% abelian groups defined by the formula
30	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
31	% \end{dfn}
32	%  [\dots]
33	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
34	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
35	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
36	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
37	% topologies  by
38	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
39	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
40	%  \end{dfn}"
41	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
42	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
43	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
44	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
45	homomorphisms.}
46	
47	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
48	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
49	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
50	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
51	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
52	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
53	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
54	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
55	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
56	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
57	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
58	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
59	structure. This is the content of
60	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
61	
62	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
63	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
64	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
65	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
66	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
67	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
68	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
69	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
70	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
71	requires on the carrier is therefore only:
72	\begin{enumerate}
73	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
74	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
75	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
76	    isomorphism classes;
77	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
78	    for \(\otimes\);
79	  \item the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
80	    (\(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)) together with the three
81	    monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\): associativity
82	    \((M \otimes N) \otimes P \cong M \otimes (N \otimes P)\), unit
83	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes M \cong M\) (and on the right), and commutativity
84	    \(M \otimes N \cong N \otimes M\). The inverse is carried by the
85	    predicate, not by a separate lemma.
86	\end{enumerate}
87	At Mathlib's pinned commit one might be tempted to obtain (3) by instantiating a
88	\emph{full} symmetric monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) and
89	reading the three isomorphisms off it. Two Mathlib facts make such a route
90	conceivable. First, the category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves
91	of modules over a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) is already a (symmetric)
92	monoidal category in Mathlib (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\)),
93	with the sectionwise relative tensor \((M \otimes_R N)(U) = M(U) \otimes_{R(U)}
94	N(U)\); this applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
95	\(R = \mathcal{O}_X\). Second, Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
96	(\(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)) takes a
97	monoidal category \(C\), a localization functor \(L : C \to D\) at a morphism
98	property \(W\), and an instance \(W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), and produces a
99	\emph{full} monoidal-category structure on the localization \(D\); sheafification
100	of presheaves of modules is the localization \(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the
101	locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\), so a full monoidal structure on
102	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) would follow once \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) were
103	supplied.
104	
105	This full-monoidal-category route is, however, \emph{not} the goal, and is not
106	pursued. The goal is the commutative \emph{group} on locally-trivial iso-classes
107	(à la \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)), and that group law consumes only propositions, so
108	it never needs a coherent monoidal category --- neither the
109	pentagon/triangle/hexagon identities nor the \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance
110	that the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) API requires. What it needs is exactly the
111	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms of (3), and these are produced
112	directly: the associator by gluing canonical local isomorphisms
113	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via the single linchpin
114	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), the unitors by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\)
115	pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}), and the braiding likewise
116	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). With those three existence facts the group law
117	assembles \emph{by hand} the commutative group of \(\otimes\)-invertible
118	\(\otimes\)-iso-classes --- the Picard group
119	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) --- each group-axiom field fed a single
120	existence-of-isomorphism. This is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
121	fixed-ring \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\),
122	whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent
123	monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) that
124	Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring; the present construction cannot take that
125	\(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route, since the coherent monoidal category on
126	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is exactly what it
127	avoids. The
128	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
129	Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) idiom) carries each inverse existentially.
130	The whole \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) / \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / stalk
131	apparatus is therefore vestigial for the group law and off the critical path.
132	
133	\section{Mathlib API survey}
134	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
135	
136	The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
137	(\texttt{b80f227}) were originally assembled into a single \emph{realization route}
138	(route~(e)): obtaining a full monoidal structure on the substrate by instantiating
139	Mathlib's abstract localization-of-monoidal-categories API rather than
140	hand-assembling the associator. That route is \emph{superseded}. The group law on
141	iso-classes never consumes a coherent monoidal category, and the associator is
142	obtained by direct gluing through \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, so the
143	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance on which route~(e) hinges is never required.
144	The survey below is retained as a Mathlib-API reference and to record why the route
145	is vestigial; the four Mathlib facts are all verified against the on-disk Mathlib
146	source.
147	
148	\paragraph{(i) The presheaf-of-modules monoidal category.} Mathlib's
149	\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal} constructs a
150	monoidal-category instance
151	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\) on
152	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,(R \circ \mathtt{forget}_2\,\mathtt{CommRingCat}\,
153	\mathtt{RingCat})\), with the sectionwise relative tensor
154	\[
155	  (M \otimes_R N)(U) \;\;=\;\; M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)
156	\]

158	% `PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory`, in
159	% `Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Presheaf/Monoidal.lean` (L104, L125)
160	% (read from .lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Presheaf/Monoidal.lean).

236	\emph{not} apply directly to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\): modules over a

238	\(A\).
239	
240	\paragraph{The gap and route~(e), superseded.} The substrate
241	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\) of this chapter is the
242	sheafification of \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\), and
243	sheafification of presheaves of modules is exactly the localization
244	\(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\). Route~(e)
245	proposed to obtain a \emph{full} monoidal structure on
246	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\)
247	by applying \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) (ii) to the already-monoidal category (i),
248	which would require the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (its two
249	whiskering-stability fields \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}, with load-bearing
250	local-injectivity half \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}). This is
251	\emph{not} how the group law is realized. The group law consumes only the
252	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms, and the associator is built
253	directly by gluing canonical local isomorphisms through
254	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}); it never invokes
255	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), a coherent monoidal category, or the stalkwise
256	whiskering apparatus. The route~(e) instance is therefore vestigial and off the
257	critical path.
258	
259	The stalk-infrastructure analysis that route~(e) turned on is recorded here for
260	reference only, since that route is superseded. The \emph{stalk module structure}
261	is already present in Mathlib: the file
262	\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk} supplies, for a topological
263	space \(X\), a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) on \(X\) and a point
264	\(x \in X\), the \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-module structure on the stalk
265	\(\mathtt{stalk}\,M.\mathtt{presheaf}\,x\) of a presheaf of modules \(M\),
266	together with the germ--scalar compatibility \(\mathtt{germ\_smul}\); a linearity
267	packaging of the induced stalk map of a morphism was built project-side
268	(\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}, the \emph{linearity half} of ingredient (d.1)).
269	Route~(e) additionally required a (d.1)-\emph{bridge} between the site-level
270	\(J.W\) and the topological stalkwise-isomorphism criterion on
271	\(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\), and (d.2) the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x
272	\otimes_{R_x} B_x\) of the stalk with the relative module-presheaf tensor; there is
273	moreover no monoidal \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) in Mathlib. Because the group law
274	is realized through the direct-gluing associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso},
275	via \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}) and never through
276	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), none of this stalk apparatus is on the critical path;
277	it is retained only to document the superseded route.
278	
279	The project's policy on this gap is \emph{project-side}: the substrate is

287	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
288	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
289	
290	\begin{definition}
291	
292	\leanok
293	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
294	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
295	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
296	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
297	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
298	  \[
299	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
300	  \]
301	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
302	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
303	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
304	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
305	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
306	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
307	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
308	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
309	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
310	  canonical compatibility
311	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
312	     \;\cong\;
313	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
314	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
315	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
316	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
317	
318	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
319	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
320	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
321	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
322	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
323	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
324	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
325	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
326	\end{definition}
327	
328	\begin{lemma}
329	
330	\leanok
331	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
332	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
333	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
334	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
335	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
336	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
337	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
338	  \[
339	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
340	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
341	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
342	  \]
343	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
344	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
345	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
346	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
347	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
348	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
349	  \[
350	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
351	    \qquad
352	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
353	  \]
354	  the associator
355	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
356	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
357	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
358	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they are off the critical path,
359	  recorded in \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
360	\end{lemma}
361	
362	\begin{proof}
363	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
364	  \leanok
365	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
366	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
367	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
368	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
369	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
370	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
371	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
372	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
373	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
374	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
375	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
376	\end{proof}
377	
378	\begin{remark}
379	[The full monoidal category on all of $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$ is off-path; the group law consumes only propositions]
380	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
381	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
382	        lem:whisker_of_W}
383	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
384	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
385	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
386	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
387	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
388	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This is explicitly \emph{off-path}
389	  and \emph{not pursued}. One could in principle obtain it as the output of
390	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) applied to the
391	  already-monoidal presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\)
392	  and the sheafification localizer \(J.W\), supplying the instance
393	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}); but the group law never
394	  consumes a coherent monoidal category, so this construction is superseded and not
395	  carried out. The substrate provides only the \emph{existence} of the three
396	  coherence isomorphisms that the group law actually consumes.
397	
398	  Were that superseded route pursued, the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\)
399	  would need \emph{no} closed structure: its
400	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) field \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\) for an
401	  \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\) is the flatness-free stalkwise statement
402	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a
403	  stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes
404	  g_x\) is again an isomorphism for any \(F\)), the same technique Mathlib uses for
405	  fixed-base presheaves in
406	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\). It would use neither
407	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) nor the
408	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (which is
409	  false over a non-affine open and is likewise off the critical path). But this
410	  apparatus is not on the critical path at all: the group law is realized without
411	  it.
412	
413	  Independently of how the monoidal structure is built, the relative Picard
414	  group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on
415	  \emph{isomorphism classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
416	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
417	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
418	  coherence data of the monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
419	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never \emph{consumed} by the
420	  group law. The group law needs only the existence of the three coherence
421	  isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
422	  lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, supplied directly: the associator by gluing canonical
423	  local isomorphisms through the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
424	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), and the
425	  unitors and braiding by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern
426	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). The carrier --- iso-classes
427	  of the substrate tensor \(\otimes_X\) --- is the scheme-theoretic analogue of
428	  Mathlib's Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
429	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}), but it
430	  is built \emph{differently}: Mathlib reads its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) off the
431	  \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent monoidal category
432	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) over a fixed ring,
433	  whereas here --- with no coherent monoidal category on
434	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) available for the varying structure sheaf --- the
435	  group is assembled by hand from the existence-of-isomorphism facts.
436	\end{remark}

438	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
439	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
440	
441	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
442	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
443	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
444	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
445	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section records the
446	\emph{existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity, unit,
447	commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law consumes. These facts
448	are supplied \emph{directly}: associativity by the direct-gluing associator
449	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} (through the linchpin
450	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), unit and commutativity by the cheap
451	\(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}),
452	and the inverse existentially from \(\otimes\)-invertibility. They are \emph{not}
453	read off any \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure: the route that would derive them from
454	\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} (route~(e)) is superseded and off-path. The section also
455	records the closure of \(\otimes\) on the locally-trivial
456	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype (\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}) and
457	the interaction with the pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). The open-immersion
458	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is on the
459	critical path precisely because it is consumed by the direct-gluing associator
460	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}; it is \emph{not} tied to the superseded
461	whiskering-stability lemma \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, which belongs
462	to the off-path route~(e).

464	\begin{lemma}
465	
466	\leanok
467	[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
468	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
469	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal}
470	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
471	  % NOTE: this CommRingCat-level lax-monoidal instance
472	  % (`PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal`, with helpers
473	  % `restrictScalarsLaxε`, `restrictScalarsLaxμ`) is a self-contained
474	  % supplement. It is NOT used by `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`, whose proof
475	  % (open-immersion sectionwise base change) identifies restriction along an
476	  % open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
477	  % needs no lax-monoidal pushforward. Retained for potential reuse; off the
478	  % critical path for the tensor group law.
479	  Let \(\varphi : R \to S\) be a morphism of presheaves of \emph{commutative}
480	  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through
481	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and the category of presheaves of modules over each
482	  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor
483	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is lax monoidal.
484	  Consequently, composing with the already-monoidal Mathlib functor
485	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) via
486	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\), the full pushforward
487	  \[
488	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
489	      \;\;:=\;\;
490	    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
491	  \]
492	  is lax monoidal. This lemma is a self-contained
493	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-level lax-monoidal supplement: it stands on its own
494	  and is retained for potential reuse, but it is \emph{off the critical path}
495	  for the tensor group law. In particular it is \emph{not} an ingredient of
496	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, whose proof identifies restriction along an
497	  open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
498	  consequently needs no lax-monoidal structure on the pushforward.
499	\end{lemma}
500	
501	\begin{proof}
502	
503	\leanok
504	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
505	  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
506	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
507	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
508	  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
509	  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\) ---
510	  i.e.\ Mathlib already uses the same mate idiom one level down). The presheaf
511	  monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,
512	  \((M_1 \otimes M_2).\mathrm{obj}\,X = M_1.\mathrm{obj}\,X \otimes M_2.\mathrm{obj}\,X\),
513	  and the presheaf functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) acts
514	  sectionwise as \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,X)\).
515	  Hence the lax structure maps \(\mu\) (laxity) and \(\varepsilon\) (unit)
516	  assemble from the per-section \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) ones, the required
517	  naturality squares being the naturality of the sectionwise maps against the
518	  presheaf restriction morphisms. Packaging these as a
519	  \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreLaxMonoidal}\) yields the lax-monoidal structure; no
520	  new diagram chase is needed beyond transporting the sectionwise coherence.
521	  The composite \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) is then lax monoidal by
522	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\) together with the monoidality of
523	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) (Mathlib), modulo a defeq
524	  transport across the definitional unfolding of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).
525	
526	  The commutativity hypothesis is essential: the presheaf-of-modules monoidal
527	  category requires \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued presheaves of rings, so the
528	  instance must be stated over \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-factored \(R, S\). In
529	  the project this holds: the relevant \(\varphi\) is
530	  \((\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\), which is
531	  structure-sheaf-valued and hence commutative-ring-valued.
532	\end{proof}

534	\begin{lemma}
535	
536	\leanok
537	[Tensor product commutes with restriction along an open immersion]
538	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
539	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
540	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
541	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be
542	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
543	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the
544	  restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison
545	  morphism
546	  \[
547	    (M \otimes_X N)\big|_f
548	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
549	    M\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; N\big|_f
550	  \]
551	  is an isomorphism. No local-freeness, flatness, or line-bundle hypothesis on
552	  \(M, N\) is required: the statement holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
553	  The reason is structural to open immersions --- restriction along \(f\) is
554	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{isomorphism} induced by \(f\),
555	  not along a general ring map --- and base change along a ring isomorphism is
556	  trivially invertible and commutes with tensor products.
557	\end{lemma}
558	
559	\begin{proof}
560	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
561	  \leanok
562	  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of
563	  rings induced by the open immersion \(f\) (concretely
564	  \(\varphi = (\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\)), so that
565	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor on
566	  presheaves of modules. The proof exploits the defining feature of an open
567	  immersion: the comparison of structure-sheaf rings over the image is an
568	  \emph{isomorphism}, not merely a ring map. For an open \(V \subseteq U\) the
569	  section ring \(\Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr)\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) over the image
570	  of \(V\) is identified with \(\Gamma(U, V)\) by the canonical \emph{ring
571	  isomorphism}
572	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V : \Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\sim}
573	  \Gamma(U, V)\)
574	  (the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) isomorphism
575	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\)). Restriction along \(f\) is
576	  therefore extension of scalars along
577	  this ring isomorphism, and the proof needs no flatness anywhere.
578	
579	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The restriction
580	  \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion \(f\) is
581	  naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
582	  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via the
583	  present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). It
584	  therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism for
585	  the abstract pullback functor.
586	
587	  \emph{Step 2 (move the pullback inside the sheafification).} The substrate
588	  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
589	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}). The pullback commutes with
590	  sheafification: there is a natural isomorphism
591	  \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
592	   \;\cong\;
593	   \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\)
594	  (the present \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\)). Hence the
595	  whole question descends to the presheaf level, where it is the base-change
596	  comparison
597	  \[
598	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(A \otimes B)
599	      \;\longrightarrow\;
600	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
601	      \;\otimes\;
602	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
603	  \]
604	  for the underlying presheaves of modules \(A, B\) of \(M\) and \(N\).
605	
606	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
607	  % \ref{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback} (the pair $(f^*, f_*)$ of
608	  % pullback/pushforward of $\mathcal{O}$-modules along a morphism of ringed
609	  % spaces is an adjoint pair)
610	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L252-273).
611	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
612	  % "\begin{lemma}
613	  % \label{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback}
614	  % Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$
615	  % be a morphism of ringed spaces.
616	  % \begin{enumerate}
617	  % \item The functor
618	  % $f_* : \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \to \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_Y)$
619	  % is left exact. In fact it commutes with all limits.
620	  % \item The functor
621	  % $f^* : \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_Y) \to \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)$
622	  % is right exact. In fact it commutes with all colimits.
623	  %  [\dots]
624	  % \end{enumerate}
625	  % \end{lemma}
626	  %  [\dots]
627	  % Parts (1) and (2) hold because $(f^*, f_*)$ is an adjoint pair
628	  % of functors, see
629	  % Sheaves, Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-adjoint-pullback-pushforward-modules}"
630	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
631	  \texttt{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback}: pullback and pushforward of
632	  $\mathcal{O}$-modules along a morphism form an adjoint pair $(f^*, f_*)$, the
633	  abstract universal property on which the comparison below rests.}
634	
635	  \emph{Step 3 (the genuine residual: H1 alone).} Completing this comparison at the
636	  presheaf level requires a sectionwise handle on the abstract functor
637	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\), which is defined as the left
638	  adjoint of pushforward (the presheaf-of-modules avatar of the
639	  \(f^* \dashv f_*\) adjunction of the cited Stacks lemma) and admits no
640	  sectionwise formula in Mathlib at the pinned commit. The closure passes through
641	  two presheaf-level ingredients, of which exactly one now remains open.
642	
643	  The first ingredient --- a strong-monoidal upgrade of
644	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along the structure-sheaf ring \emph{isomorphism}
645	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\), so that base change along the isomorphism commutes with
646	  \(\otimes\) --- is \emph{closed in this chapter} as
647	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
648	  (\(\mathtt{restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv}\)): for a ring isomorphism
649	  \(e : R \simeq S\) the canonical map \(a \otimes_R b \mapsto a \otimes_S b\) is an
650	  \(R\)-linear equivalence \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M) \otimes_R
651	  \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(N) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M
652	  \otimes_S N)\).
653	
654	  The \emph{sole remaining residual} is therefore the presheaf-level identification
655	  \[
656	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \;\cong\; \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
657	  \]
658	  via \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\). One exhibits a
659	  presheaf-level adjunction

661	  that \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\), being a left adjoint of
662	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\), is canonically isomorphic to the abstract left
663	  adjoint \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\). Here \(\beta\) is the structure map of the
664	  restriction functor, so that \((M|_f).\mathrm{val} =
665	  (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathrm{obj}\,M.\mathrm{val}\) definitionally;
666	  composing the resulting comparison with
667	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv} yields the asserted isomorphism.
668	
669	  This presheaf-level adjunction is the presheaf analogue of the sheaf-level
670	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\), and must be built from
671	  presheaf-level \(\mathtt{pushforwardNatTrans}\) and
672	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardCongr}\). These two building blocks are \emph{Mathlib-absent}

675	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardId}\) and \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\) do already exist at the
676	  presheaf level. This residual is being constructed \emph{directly}, mirroring the
677	  existing sheaf-level template app-for-app at the presheaf level (a Mathlib-gradient
678	  build, not a deferral to an upstream PR).
679	
680	  This lemma is the single substrate linchpin of the relative Picard group law for
681	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(M, N\): the associator
682	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} consumes the comparison

862	  results about flat whiskering of sheafification-localizer morphisms. They are,

864	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (route~(e)) are the flatness-free variants

876	\begin{proof}
877	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
878	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of the sheafification localizer
879	  (\(J.W\) is exactly the class of morphisms that are \(J\)-locally injective and
880	  \(J\)-locally surjective), it suffices to show that \(F \triangleleft g\) is
881	  both locally injective and locally surjective whenever \(g\) is.
882	
883	  The presheaf-of-modules tensor product is computed sectionwise,
884	  \((F \otimes^{p} G)(U) = F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} G(U)\), so for
885	  \(g : A \to B\) the whiskered morphism acts on sections over \(U\) as the
886	  \(R(U)\)-linear map
887	  \[
888	    \mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U) \;\colon\;
889	      F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} A(U) \;\longrightarrow\; F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U).
890	  \]
891	
892	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is automatic and needs no hypothesis on \(F\):
893	  tensoring is right exact, so applying \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the cokernel
894	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\); since \(g\) is locally
895	  surjective its cokernel is locally zero, hence so is the cokernel of
896	  \(F \triangleleft g\), and \(F \triangleleft g\) is locally surjective.
897	
898	  \emph{Local injectivity} is where flatness enters. Because each \(F(U)\) is a
899	  flat \(R(U)\)-module, the functor \(F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} -\) preserves injective
900	  linear maps (Mathlib
901	  \(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\)). Thus
902	  whenever \(g(U)\) is injective on a covering sieve, so is
903	  \((\mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U))\); the local injectivity of \(g\) is
904	  therefore transported sectionwise to \(F \triangleleft g\).
905	
906	  Locally injective and locally surjective together give
907	  \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
908	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
909	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, which is an
910	  isomorphism and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\); the flatness of \(F\) is
911	  the only input, and it is symmetric in the two tensor factors.
912	\end{proof}

1096	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
1097	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the sheafification localizer (the locally
1098	  bijective morphisms). Concretely the site carries the
1099	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) property for \(\mathtt{Ab}\), so \(J.W\) is
1100	  exactly the class of \(J\)-locally bijective morphisms; this is recorded as a
1101	  typeclass hypothesis \([J.\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\,\mathtt{Ab}]\) on
1102	  the statement. Let \(F\) be an \emph{arbitrary} presheaf of
1103	  \(R\)-modules, and let \(g : M \to N\) be a morphism of presheaves of
1104	  \(R\)-modules whose underlying morphism of presheaves of abelian groups
1105	  \((\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R).\mathrm{map}\,g\) lies in \(J.W\). Then the
1106	  left-whiskered morphism
1107	  \[
1108	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
1109	  \]
1110	  is \emph{\(J\)-locally injective}. No flatness and no local-triviality
1111	  hypothesis on \(F\) is required.
1112	
1113	  This is the \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\)-field content (the injectivity half) of the
1114	  instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}), the load-bearing
1115	  field of the \emph{full-generality} route~(e) \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) path
1116	  (arbitrary \(F\)). For the relative Picard group law, however, the whisker object
1117	  \(F\) supplied by the sole consumer --- the locally-trivial-scoped associator
1118	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} --- is always \emph{locally trivial}
1119	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,F\)), and in that case local injectivity
1120	  of \(F \triangleleft g\) admits a markedly simpler, stalk-free proof, given as the
1121	  PRIMARY route below. The general stalkwise argument (ingredients (d.1)/(d.2)) is
1122	  retained only as the FALLBACK, required if the full route~(e) monoidal category on
1123	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) is later wanted; the locally-trivial
1124	  primary route already suffices for the group law.
1125	\end{lemma}

1132	  no stalks, no (d.2)).} Suppose \(F\) is locally trivial
1133	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,F\)), which is exactly the hypothesis its
1134	  sole consumer --- the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} --- supplies.
1135	  \(J\)-local injectivity of a morphism is \emph{local on a covering family}: it
1136	  suffices to produce a cover of \(X\) on each member of which \(F \triangleleft g\)
1137	  restricts to a locally injective morphism. Take a trivialising open cover
1138	  \(\{V\}\) of \(X\) for \(F\), so that \(F|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) on each \(V\).
1139	  On such a \(V\):
1140	  \begin{itemize}
1141	    \item the substrate--restriction compatibility \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1142	      gives a canonical isomorphism
1143	      \[
1144	        (F \triangleleft g)\big|_V \;\cong\; (F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V)
1145	      \]
1146	      --- restriction along the open immersion \(V \hookrightarrow X\) commutes with
1147	      \(\otimes\), and the whiskering \(F \triangleleft g = \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g\)
1148	      is \(\otimes\) with an identity, so it restricts to \(\mathrm{id}_{F|_V}
1149	      \otimes (g|_V) = (F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V)\);
1150	    \item the trivialisation \(F|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) together with the left
1151	      unitor \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} (\(\mathcal{O}_V \otimes_V (-) \cong
1152	      (-)\)) identifies \((F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V) \cong g|_V\).
1153	  \end{itemize}
1154	  Composing, \((F \triangleleft g)|_V\) is isomorphic to \(g|_V\). Now \(g \in J.W\),
1155	  and membership in \(J.W\) (local bijectivity) is stable under restriction along an
1156	  open immersion, so \(g|_V \in J.W\); in particular \(g|_V\) is \(J\)-locally
1157	  injective. An isomorphism preserves local injectivity, hence \((F \triangleleft
1158	  g)|_V\) is locally injective on \(V\). As the \(\{V\}\) cover \(X\) and local
1159	  injectivity is local on the cover, \(F \triangleleft g\) is \(J\)-locally
1160	  injective. \emph{No stalks and no (d.2) stalk--tensor commutation enter}; the
1161	  single comparison lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (a presheaf-level
1162	  comparison, decomposed into its two open-immersion base-change halves) carries the
1163	  argument, and thereby moves \emph{onto} the critical path.
1164	
1165	  This is \emph{not} the section-level route that tensors a bare section map \(g(V)\)
1166	  --- only injective --- and stalls on a \(\mathrm{Tor}_1\)/flatness obligation. Here
1167	  the argument is sheaf-level and uses that \(g\) is locally \emph{bijective} (a
1168	  \(J.W\) morphism), reduced through the unitor to \(g|_V\) (again a \(J.W\)
1169	  morphism); a mere injection is never tensored, so no flatness is needed.
1170

1172	  full-generality route~(e) \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) path).} For an

1174	  proved stalkwise, by the argument Mathlib uses for fixed-base presheaves in
1175	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\)
1176	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}), ported to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\). A
1177	  morphism in \(J.W\) is, on the topological site of \(X\), a \emph{stalkwise
1178	  isomorphism}: at each point \(x\) the induced map on stalks \(g_x\) is an
1179	  isomorphism. The presheaf-of-modules tensor is sectionwise, so it commutes with
1180	  the stalk (a filtered colimit), giving
1181	  \[

1184	      \;\colon\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} M_x \;\longrightarrow\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} N_x .
1185	  \]

1187	  isomorphism --- no flatness is needed, since \(g_x\) is invertible, not merely
1188	  injective. Hence \(F \triangleleft g\) is a stalkwise isomorphism, in particular
1189	  locally bijective, in particular locally injective, for \emph{any} \(F\).

1250	  \emph{arbitrary} presheaf of \(R\)-modules \(F\) and a morphism \(g\) whose
1251	  underlying additive-presheaf map lies in \(J.W\), both whiskerings again lie in
1252	  \(J.W\):
1253	  \[
1254	    g \in J.W \;\Longrightarrow\; F \triangleleft g \in J.W,
1255	    \qquad
1256	    g \in J.W \;\Longrightarrow\; g \triangleright F \in J.W .
1257	  \]
1258	  These two statements are precisely the \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) and
1259	  \(\mathtt{whiskerRight}\) data required by the class
1260	  \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}) for
1261	  the morphism property \(J.W\); together they furnish the instance
1262	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) of \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}. No flatness and no
1263	  local triviality is used --- they are the flatness-free replacements for the
1264	  flat variants of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}.
1265	\end{lemma}

1267	\begin{proof}
1268	  \uses{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1269	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of \(J.W\), it suffices to show
1270	  \(F \triangleleft g\) is both locally injective and locally surjective.
1271	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is free and needs no hypothesis on \(F\): the presheaf
1272	  tensor is right exact, so \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the locally-zero cokernel
1273	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\), which is therefore locally
1274	  zero (Mathlib \(\mathtt{isLocallySurjective\_whiskerLeft}\)). \emph{Local
1275	  injectivity} is \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}. The two together
1276	  give \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
1277	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
1278	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf monoidal structure, which is an isomorphism
1279	  and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\).
1280	\end{proof}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
  Locally trivial of rank one is preserved by
  \(\mathcal{H}om(-, \mathcal{O}_X)\): on a common affine trivialising
  cover, the dual of a free rank-one module is again free of rank one.
  The contraction map
  \(L \otimes_X \mathcal{H}om(L, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is a
  morphism in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), and on each open of the
  trivialising cover it specialises to the contraction
  \(A \otimes_A A \to A\) of free rank-one \(A\)-modules, which is an
  isomorphism; hence the contraction is a global isomorphism. The
  abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(X) := \texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim\)
  is then standard (Stacks tag 01CR).
\end{proof}
input.new_string:
\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso,
        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
  Set \(L^{-1} := \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\).

  \emph{Step 1 (\(L^{-1}\) is a line bundle).} Local triviality of \(L\)
  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,L\)) furnishes an open cover \(\{U\}\) of
  \(X\) with trivialisations \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). The internal hom commutes
  with restriction along the open immersion \(U \hookrightarrow X\), so on each \(U\)
  \[
    L^{-1}\big|_U \;\cong\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_U}(L|_U, \mathcal{O}_U)
                 \;\cong\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_U}(\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{O}_U)
                 \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_U ,
  \]
  the last identification being the evaluation-at-\(1\) isomorphism for the dual of a
  free rank-one module. Hence \(L^{-1}\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an
  object of \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).

  \emph{Step 2 (the contraction is a local isomorphism).} The evaluation/contraction
  morphism
  \[
    \varepsilon_L \;\colon\; L \otimes_X L^{-1}
      \;=\; L \otimes_X \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)
      \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_X,
    \qquad s \otimes \phi \longmapsto \phi(s),
  \]
  is a morphism in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Restricting to a trivialising
  open \(U\), the restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
  commutes \(\otimes_X\) past \((-)|_U\):
  \[
    (L \otimes_X L^{-1})\big|_U \;\cong\; L|_U \otimes_U L^{-1}|_U
      \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U \mathcal{O}_U ,
  \]
  using the trivialisations of Step~1. Under these identifications \(\varepsilon_L|_U\)
  becomes the contraction \(\mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U \mathcal{O}_U \to \mathcal{O}_U\) of
  the free rank-one module with its dual, which is precisely the left unitor
  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} (\(\mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U (-) \cong (-)\)) and hence an
  isomorphism. Thus \(\varepsilon_L|_U\) is an isomorphism on each member of the cover.

  \emph{Step 3 (glue to a global isomorphism).} The local trivialisations are induced
  by the open immersions with no choices, so the local contraction isomorphisms
  \(\varepsilon_L|_U\) agree on the overlaps \(U \cap U'\) by the naturality of
  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} and of the unitor under further restriction. As
  ``being an isomorphism'' is local on \(X\) --- a morphism of sheaves of
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules whose restriction to every member of an open cover is an
  isomorphism is itself an isomorphism --- the globally-defined \(\varepsilon_L\) is an
  isomorphism \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\). Equivalently, the
  local inverses glue (they agree on overlaps for the same reason) to a two-sided
  inverse of \(\varepsilon_L\).

  Consequently every line bundle \(L\) has the two-sided tensor inverse \(L^{-1}\) under
  \(\otimes_X\), and the monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes of line bundles is an abelian
  group --- the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag 01CR).
\end{proof}
